retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary ciliary dysfunction is a genetic disorder that results in abnormal ciliary ultrastructure or function. Currently, 40 known genes are associated with primary ciliary dysfunction, with the majority following autosomal recessive inheritance. [4] DNAI1 and DNAH5 , which encode components of the outer dynein arm complex in cilia, are the 2 most common genes associated with PCD. [4] Two rare X-linked genes, RPGR and OFD1, have also been identified. [8] Different mutations, even to the same gene, can result in varying degrees of loss of function of the cilia, further contributing to the heterogeneity of the disease. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Spermatogenesis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of the sperm tail closely resembles the motile cilium in that the axoneme has a 9+2 microtubular arrangement. Therefore, genetic defects detected in motile cilia can significantly affect the formation of the sperm tail. A genetic disease called PCD (primary ciliary dyskinesia) due to the malformation of motile cilia causes pulmonary disease, increased risk of infections, and male infertility. Many genes are associated with PCD; however, the exact effect these mutated genes have on spermatogenesis is still investigational. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Neural Tube -- Molecular Level -- Cilia
retrieved_docs.content: These are hair-like structures that help to regulate signal transduction with the use of mechanical molecular cues. Cilia are essential in major signaling pathways such as the sonic hedgehog pathway during neural tube formation. Approximately 5% of neural tube disorders have a relation to genes that are responsible for cilia formation and function. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, false positives can occur in all the above testing methods due to secondary loss of cilia function. This can occur, for example, if the sample was taken during an acute viral illness that caused transient loss of function. Cell culture can be used to minimize this impact. [25] Genetic testing should also be considered, as 33 genes have been associated with primary ciliary dyskinesia. However, not all of them are included in the commercially available primary ciliary dyskinesia genetic testing panel. As genetic panels continue to develop and advance, genetic testing continues to be a valued test for diagnosis and research. Currently, however, there remain limitations as known PCD patients can have normal genetic testing. [16] In the United States, a combination of ciliary biopsy to demonstrate ciliary ultrastructural abnormalities and genetic testing is used for the diagnosis. In contrast, a combination of genetic testing and high-speed video microscopy is used in Europe.

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cilia exist on epithelial cells lining the nasopharynx, lower respiratory tract, paranasal sinuses, middle ear, and reproductive tract. [2] [4] In primary ciliary dysfunction, mutations in genes encoding for axonemal structures of motile cilia result in cilia that are abnormal in structure and function. Defects can cause outer dynein arm defects, inner dynein arm defects, central microtubular abnormalities, radial spoke defects, and other ultrastructural abnormalities. [2] [3] [9] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A dysfunctional protein can lead to a variety of medical conditions and, often, death. Dysfunctional proteins can lead to  childhood obesity and the breakdown of the retina, leading to blindness, hearing loss, and type 2 diabetes. Take, for example, the protein cilia and how its dysfunction manifests. Inadequate cilia in flagella lead to sperm dysmotility. Defective cilia in the respiratory tract lead to chronic lung infections. Eustachian tube cilia dysfunction causes chronic ear infections and hearing loss. Dysfunctional cilia in the fallopian tubes cause infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Advances in electron microscopy have enabled a deeper understanding of the structural consequences of genetic variations. [5] Although electron microscopy (EM) analysis can aid the diagnosis, a subset of patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia with no demonstrable ultra-structural abnormalities were identified. As such, electron microscopy can have a false negative rate of 20%-30%. Negative results need to be reviewed in the context of the entire clinical picture. [12] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Histidine -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Histidine demonstrates diverse roles in cellular function. In addition to playing a structural and catalytic role in many enzymes, histidine residues can undergo enzyme-catalyzed methylation (using S-adenosyl methionine as the methyl donor), as illustrated by the critical role of 3-methylhistidine in the ATP binding site of actin. [7] Histidine residues are also crucial to maintaining the myelin sheath as they participate in the hydroxylation of the galactosylceramide, which is responsible for the compaction of the myelin. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer-Associated Retinopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ohguro et al suggested that humoral autoimmunity against recoverin and heat shock cognate protein 70 (65 kDa) may play a role in the pathogenesis of CAR. [51] Other antigens associated with CAR include: Anticarbonic anhydrase II (30 kDa) [52] Anti–glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) Interphotoreceptor retinoid-binding protein (145 kDa) [53] Neurofilament proteins [35] Tubby-like protein 1 [TULP1] [54] Arrestin Heat shock cognate (HSC) protein 70 Guanylyl cyclase–activating proteins (GCAP) 1 and 2 Photoreceptor-specific nuclear receptor 9PNR) Rab6A GTPase (Rab6A) and other unidentified antibodies formed against the retinal proteins

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome: is an autosomal dominant disease due to mutations involving the BHD gene, which transcribes folliculin. The syndrome manifests a range of histological types for renal tumors. Associated features include fibrofolliculomas, trichodiscomas, and acrochordons. Pulmonary cysts may also be seen. [8] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis -- Etiology -- Genetic Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
retrieved_docs.content: Several genes encoding proteins involved in the slit diaphragm, cell membrane, cytoskeleton, nuclear, mitochondrial, and lysosomal functions have been identified as abnormal, leading to a loss of integrity in the glomerular filtration barrier and resulting in FSGS. [12] Examples include mutations in NPHS1 and NHPS2 , which lead to the absence of essential slit diaphragm components nephrin or podocin, leading to severe congenital nephrotic syndrome. Additionally, polymorphisms in APOL1 , commonly found in individuals of African descent, significantly increase the risk of developing FSGS. In fact, approximately 75% of Black patients with FSGS carry a high-risk APOL1 mutation. [13] These patients often present with collapsing FSGS, which rapidly progresses to end-stage renal disease (ESRD). [4] [14] Other disease-causing mutations occur, affecting proteins such as nephrin, podocin, α-actinin-4, and β-integrin. Patients with genetic FSGS may have a relevant family history, and they often present at a young age. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Primary ciliary dysfunction (PCD), first described in 1976, is a disorder of the structure and function of motile cilia that results in chronic oto-sinopulmonary disease. [1] Cilia are hair-like structures that exist on the surface of cells. [2] Motile cilia exist throughout the upper and lower respiratory tract, with each respiratory epithelial cell containing hundreds of cilia. Clearance of the airway and movement of respiratory secretions depends on the cilia beating in a coordinated fashion. [2] [3] PCD is a heterogeneous genetic condition that causes various abnormalities in the axonemal structures that comprise the cilia. This subsequently results in abnormal cilia structure and function. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Etiology -- Genetic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal Dominant: CDON gene-related Steinfield syndrome FGFR1 gene-related Kallman syndrome and Hartsfield syndrome (holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, and ectrodactyly) Agnathia-Otocephaly Complex (gene PRRX1) Culler-Jones syndrome CHARGE syndrome Autosomal Recessive: CENPF gene-related Stromme syndrome DHCR7 gene-related Smith-Lemli-Opitz Syndrome - intellectual disability, dysmorphisms of the face, congenital cardiac anomalies, and male external genitalia defects [12] [13] HHAT (encoding Hedgehog acyltransferase) PLCH1 (encoding phospholipase C eta-1) STIL [encoding a factor required for maintenance of primary cilia) [3] Hydrolethalus syndrome ( HYLS1 and KIF7 genes ) Genoa syndrome Lambotte syndrome X-linked: X-linked holoprosencephaly-13 ( HPE13 ) ( STAG2 ) [14] Variants in genes: Driver genes: Essential to the phenotype of the patient carrying a variant- SHH , ZIC2 , SIX3 , FGF8 , and FGFR1 Silent modifier gene: BOC Predisposing genes: All other genes responsible

retrieved_docs.title: Filarial Hydrocele -- Treatment / Management -- World Health Organization–Recommended Preventive Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Albendazole: Albendazole causes selective degeneration of cytoplasmic microtubules in parasites and their larvae by binding to the B-tubulin microtubular subunit, thereby blocking microtubule growth and polymerization. [91] [92] [93] Albendazole inhibits the parasite's glucose metabolism and reduces its glycogen storage reserves. [93] At higher concentrations, it can also halt the Krebs cycle by inhibiting key enzymes such as malate dehydrogenase. [91] [92] [93]  This inhibition drastically reduces ATP production, resulting in reduced energy, immobilization, and the death of the parasite. [91] [92] [93] Albendazole is effective against both adult nematodes and microfilariae; however, its effects are gradual, so adverse effects from the rapid killing of parasites are not typically observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cystic Disease -- Etiology -- Inherited Renal Cystic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Glomerulocystic kidney disease is primarily found in neonates and infants with a family history of PKD, but not all cases are associated with mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 . This condition is associated with cyst formation in the glomerulus and increased Bowman's space due to gene mutations in renal tubular primary cilia or the centrosome. About 10% of cases are associated with mutations in HFN1β , and this phenotype results in hypoplastic kidneys with defects in the renal calyces and collecting systems. Of note, HFN1β is thought to regulate transcription of PKD2 and PKHD1 . [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: antitrichomonal agents are connected to hachimycin, indicating a therapeutic or mechanistic association. Collectively, the taxonomy highlights that antiprotozoal agents are employed against parasites and single‑cell infections and have therapeutic relevance across bacterial, fungal, protozoal and parasitic diseases, providing a structured framework for clinical decision‑making and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1046
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyquinoline derivatives for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases are classified as members of the broxyquinoline group and are identified as agents used to treat amoebiasis and other protozoal infections. This categorization highlights their role as therapeutic compounds specifically targeting protozoal pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorchinaldol is classified as a member of the hydroxyquinoline derivatives used for the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, indicating its placement within this therapeutic drug group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1354
retrieved_docs.content: Broxyquinoline is classified as a member of the hydroxyquinoline derivatives used for the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorchinaldol and broxyquinoline are classified as hydroxyquinoline derivatives that are employed in the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, indicating that both agents belong to the therapeutic group of hydroxyquinoline compounds used for managing these parasitic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Demethylchlortetracyklin, a tetracycline‑derived antimicrobial agent, may be used to prevent عدوى وحيدات الخلية (infections caused by single‑celled organisms). This relationship underscores the potential prophylactic role of demethylchlortetracyklin in managing unicellular infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1381
retrieved_docs.content: Demethylchlortetracyklin is classified as a member of the tetracycline and derivatives topical antibiotics group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: classified under the parent term obinadla. Hydroxyquinoline derivatives used for amoebiasis include broxyquinoline, and the therapeutic class quinoline gynecological anti‑infectives comprises broxyquinoline and dijodhydroxychin for treatment of gynecological infections. Finally, the dermaseptin peptide is derived from the kationické antimikrobiální peptidy family, which in turn is mapped from the magainin 1 peptide originally isolated from Xenopus species. Together, these hierarchical therapeutic categories, formulation linkages, and peptide‑based antimicrobial families provide clinicians and pharmacists with a structured framework for selecting, combining and developing antimicrobial, anti‑infective, antihistaminic, anti‑parasitic, wound‑care and related therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Other agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases in ATC is defined as a subclass of agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, and the specific compound chinakrin is identified as a member of this subclass, indicating its classification among the other agents used to treat amoebiasis and related protozoal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1692
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorchinaldol and broxyquinoline are both classified as hydroxyquinoline derivatives indicated for the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, and they are also members of the quinoline gynecological anti‑infective group, reflecting their dual utility in protozoal and gynecological infections. Dijodhydroxychin is likewise a member of the quinoline gynecological anti‑infective class, underscoring its role as a quinoline‑based agent used primarily in gynecological anti‑infective therapy. Together, these compounds illustrate how quinoline‑based anti‑infectives can be organized into therapeutic categories that span both protozoal disease management and gynecological infection treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyquinoline derivatives for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases are classified as agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, indicating their primary use in treating protozoal infections. Within this therapeutic class, both chlorchinaldol and broxyquinoline are identified as members, reflecting their activity against amoebiasis and related protozoal conditions. These same agents—chlorchinaldol and broxyquinoline—are also members of the quinoline gynecological antiinfectives group, demonstrating an overlap in their antimicrobial spectrum that extends to gynecological infections. In addition, the quinoline gynecological antiinfectives category includes dijodhydroxychin, which is specifically associated with gynecological anti‑infective applications. This shared membership highlights the multifunctional nature of certain quinoline‑based compounds, which can be employed both for protozoal disease management and for treating gynecological infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Antitrichomonádové látky, which are agents used to treat infections caused by Trichomonas parasites, are associated with hachimycin, a specific compound that belongs to this class of antitrichomonal medications. This relationship highlights hachimycin as an example of an antitrichomonádové látka employed in the therapeutic management of trichomoniasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: Arsenic compounds, for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases are classified as agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, indicating their therapeutic role in treating these infections. Hydroxyquinoline derivatives for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases are also agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, and they belong to the broxyquinoline group. Together, these arsenic‑based agents and hydroxyquinoline‑based agents represent pharmacologic options used to combat amoebiasis and a range of other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrofuran derivatives, classified as antiprotozoal drugs, function as therapeutic agents against the protozoal infections leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis, providing targeted treatment options for these diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyklin is an antibiotic that exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its impact on cellular proliferation. This property, together with its antimicrobial activity, makes oxytetracyklin a therapeutic option for treating داء الريكتسيات (rickettsial disease). Understanding both the antiproliferative effect and the antimicrobial efficacy of oxytetracyklin informs its appropriate use in managing infections caused by rickettsial organisms.
